narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at past students' profiles—like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict if they'd pass the bar exam on their first try, and it flagged your profile as higher risk. Specifically, it estimated a 58% chance that you might not pass the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for denial since the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is much lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile for others—suggesting your later academic performance stood out as a concern compared to most students. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, just below the average 3.21, actually helped nudge the prediction toward success more than many others' did. Your plan to attend part-time, which is less common (only about 7% of students choose this versus nearly all going full-time), contributed somewhat toward the failure risk, as full-time paths have historically led to stronger bar outcomes. Your LSAT score of 27, well below the usual 37, offered a small counterbalance toward passing, while your middle family income bracket was a minor positive influence, aligning with averages. Your Year 1 decile of 3rd had only a slight push toward failure risk. Overall, compared to typical admitted students who boast stronger late-stage grades and full-time commitments, your profile highlighted vulnerabilities in those key areas. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year academics perhaps through extra tutoring, and exploring full-time options if feasible, then reapply with those improvements to shift the model's view. You've got resilience on your side; keep pushing forward.
